policy_statement: Integrate lower-carbon materials and deconstruction into City incentive programs (e.g., expedited permitting and Austin Energy Green Building) and transition voluntary guidance into agreements over time.
mechanism_description: Incentives and development agreements encourage lower-carbon materials and deconstruction practices, reducing embodied emissions from construction and renovation activities.
secondary_justification: Resource Efficiency: Lower-embodied-carbon strategies commonly and inherently involve using less virgin material and/or substituting with reused/recycled/low-impact alternatives and deconstruction (material recovery), which reduces material throughput for delivering the same built floor area/service.
primary_causal_pathway: Building design/construction choices → ↓ embodied emissions from material supply chains (cement/steel/wood/etc.) → ↓ total lifecycle GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change: Builders/developers and city programs shift specifications and procurement toward low-carbon materials (e.g., lower-clinker cement, mass timber where appropriate, high-recycled-content steel), require/encourage embodied-carbon accounting and caps, and adopt deconstruction/material reuse practices.  
(2) Process: These changes reduce process emissions (e.g., calcination in cement), fossil fuel combustion in manufacturing, and upstream supply-chain emissions by lowering carbon intensity per unit material and/or reducing virgin material demand through reuse/recycling.  
(3) Outcome: Net reduction in CO2e emitted during building construction and major renovations (cradle-to-gate and potentially end-of-life stages), lowering community-wide GHG inventories.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced greenhouse gas emissions from the production and use of construction materials (lower embodied carbon), which lowers overall construction-related lifecycle emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: altering construction materials/practices to reduce embodied carbon directly lowers GHG emissions from industrial/material supply chains.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: the core intent and largest climate effect is emissions reduction (not hazard reduction or ecosystem restoration); energy-use reductions in operations are not the main lever here.  
3) Inherent secondary causal chain(s): Resource Efficiency is inherent because embodied-carbon reduction in buildings is physically achieved in many cases by reducing virgin material throughput via reuse/deconstruction/recycled content and more material-efficient design; that is a same-service/less-material-input pathway that accompanies the embodied-carbon objective.  
4) Edge cases applied: some low-carbon substitutions could increase material mass or shift impacts (e.g., heavier assemblies, different sourcing), but the mechanism as defined still targets net embodied GHG reductions; adaptation benefits (e.g., resilience) are optional and not guaranteed.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: any policy using this mechanism is fundamentally aimed at lowering construction lifecycle CO2e (Mitigation); material-throughput reduction/reuse is a common and mechanism-linked efficiency channel, so Resource Efficiency can reasonably be treated as inherent to the embodied-carbon-reduction approach as expressed (including deconstruction/material best practices).
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a policy pursues “low-carbon materials” solely via fuel-switching/renewable energy at factories without changing material efficiency or reuse, the Resource Efficiency secondary may be weaker; similarly, some bio-based material choices can implicate land-use/carbon storage, but those effects are not universal enough here to classify as Nature-Based Solutions.
key_indicators: “Incentivize lower-carbon materials”, “integrate … into City incentive programs”, “expedited permitting process”, “Austin Energy’s Green Building program”, “deconstruction practices”, “transition voluntary design guidance into planning and development agreements”
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits include reduced construction and demolition waste to landfill, local job creation in deconstruction/material salvage, and reduced conventional air pollution from lower-emissions material production; however these are not explicitly framed as separate mechanism-level categories in the text.
instance_edge_case_notes: “Transition voluntary guidance into planning and development agreements” could evolve toward a planning/contractual requirement over time, but as written the operative near-term mechanism is incentive-based (program integration), not an explicit mandate.
